Existence of superdecomposable pure-injective modules reflects complexity in the category of finite-dimensional representations over an algebra. Such an existence occurswhenanalgebraisnon-domestic;aconjectureduetoM.Prest. G.Puniskiconfirms the conjecture for non-domestic string algebras. Geiß, Labardini-Fragoso and Schro ̈er showthateveryJacobianalgebraassociatedwithatriangulationofaclosedsurfacewith marked points is finite-dimensional and tame. We show that, excluding only the case of a sphere with four (or fewer) punctures, there exists a special family of pointed modules, called an independent pair of dense chains of pointed modules. In the process, we show the existence of such an independent pair in a non-domestic skew-gentle algebra and (skew)BrauergraphalgebrasbyshowingthattheGaloissemi-coveringfunctorandtrivial extensionpreservesuchpairs. Drozd’stheoremin[5] states a well-known dichotomy that every finite-dimensional algebra over an algebraically closed field K is either tame or wild (see definitions in [41]) but not both. The class of tame algebras may be stratified with respect to the growth of a tame algebra, thanks to A. Skowron ́ski. This stratification distinguishes the classes of algebras of domestic, linear, polynomial and exponential growth in [40]. The growth of a tame algebra Λ reflects the complexity of the category Λ-mod of all finite-dimensional left Λ-modules. AnindependentpairofdensechainsofpointedΛ-modulesallowsustoformulateahandy sufficient condition for the existence of a super-decomposable (i.e., without indecomposable directsummands)pure-injectivemodule. M.Ziegler[44]establishedafundamentalexisting criterion for such modules, which states that a countable ring Λ has a super-decomposable pure-injective module if and only if the width of the lattice of all pp-formulae is undefined. The later statement can be formulated in terms of the width of the lattice of all pointed finitelypresentedΛ-modules. Theexistenceofsuchmodulescouldassistwiththeclassifica- tion problem as described in the following conjectures due to Prest. The first one is that Λ is tame if and only if it does not possess a super decomposable pure-injective module. The second conjecture [25] states that an algebra Λ is of domestic representation type iff there is no super-decomposable pure-injective module. G. Here, we show the existence of such a module over sev- eral tame algebras-some Jacobian algebras, non-domestic skew-gentle algebras, their trivial extension algebras and (skew-) Brauer graph algebras over a countable field.

Some cases with KG(Λ) = ∞: Here is a list of algebras where the width of the lattice of all pointed modules is infinite. As a result, their KG dimension is undefined. M. Prest [26]provesthatsuper-decomposablepure-injectivemodulesexistoverstrictlywildalgebras, whereas the existence is shown in wild algebras by L. Gregory and M. Prest [11]. In [21], G. Puninski shows that super-decomposable pure-injectives exist over string algebras of non- polynomial growth if the base field is countable. Later, he provide a explicit construction of such a module in [22] for some string algebra of non-polynomial growth over an arbitrary field. R. Harland proves in [15] that such a module exists for tubular algebras. S. Kasjan and G. Pastuszak show in [18] that there are super-decomposable pure-injective modules over all strongly simply connected K-algebras of non-polynomial growth. The main result of [23] asserts that if Λ is a strongly simply connected K-algebra and K is countable, then Λ is of non-domestic type if and only if there exists a super-decomposable pure-injective Λ-module. The representation-infinite domestic standard self-injective algebras over al- gebraically closed fields do not have super-decomposable pure-injective modules (see [24, Theorem 8.3]). All the known results support the second conjecture of Prest concerning super-decomposable pure-injective modules. Here, we enlarge the list by showing the exis- tence of an independent pair of dense chains of pointed modules and a super-decomposable pure-injective module over non-domestic skew-gentle algebras and some Jacobian algebras. A potential W for a quiver Q is, roughly speaking, a linear combination of cyclic paths in the complete path algebra K⟨⟨Q⟩⟩. The Jacobian algebra P(Q,W) associated with a quiver with a potential (Q,W) is the quotient of the complete path algebra K⟨⟨Q⟩⟩ modulo the Jacobian ideal J(W). Here, J(W) is the topological closure of the ideal of K⟨⟨Q⟩⟩ which is generated by the cyclic derivatives of W with respect to the arrows of Q. Quivers with potential were introduced in [6] to construct additive categorifications of cluster algebras with skew-symmetric exchange matrix. For that, the potential for Q must be non-degenerate, i.e., it can be mutated along with the quiver arbitrarily. In [9], the authors introduced, under some mild hypothesis, for each oriented surface with marked points (S,M), a mutation finite cluster algebra with skew-symmetric exchange matrices. More precisely, each triangulation T of (S,M) by tagged arcs corresponds to a cluster and the corresponding exchange matrix is conveniently coded into a quiver Q(T).

Labardini- Fragoso in [19] enhanced this construction by introducing potentials W(T) and showed thatthesepotentialsarecompatiblewithmutations. Inparticular,thesepotentialsarenon- degenerate. Ladkani[20]showedthatforsurfaceswithemptyboundaryandatriangulation T that has no self-folded triangles, the Jacobian algebra P(Q(T),W(T)) is symmetric (and, in particular, finite-dimensional). It follows that for any triangulation T′ of a closed surface (S,M), the Jacobian algebra P(Q(T′ ),W(T′ )) is weakly symmetric [16]. In [13], the authors use a degeneration argument to show that these algebras are tame. Moreover, Y. Valdivieso-D ́ıaz [43] showed that these algebras, except for the case of a sphere with four (or fewer) punctures, exhibit exponential growth. The algebras associated with a sphere with five punctures are associated with a quotient of a skew-gentle algebra, and we produce a ‘non-symmetric’ independent dense pair of pointed modules (See Definition 3.6) over the associated gentle algebra (Theorem 4.4), which is non-domestic (Proposition 4.2), whereas in the rest of the cases, they are presented as the quotient of non-domestic string algebras (Proposition 4.1). We show the existence of an independent pair of dense chains of pointed modules over a skew group algebra when the given algebra has a non-symmetric one. We end the section with the statement that the Jacobian algebras have a superdecomposable module and their KG-dimension is undefined (Theorem 4.5 and Corollary 4.6). In Section (5), we explicitly show the existence of superdecomposable modules over some well-known algebras like the incidence algebra of the Nazarova–Zavadskij poset, diamond algebra, a garland and some surface algebra as an application of Theorem 3.7. Finally, in Section (6), we show that a superdecomposable module over an algebra is preserved when we consider its trivial (or relation) extension algebra (Theorem 6.2), but the converse is not true in general. We produce a counter-example (Example 6.7) of a Brauer graph algebra, as it is obtained as a trivial extension algebra over a gentle algebra. Moreover, we comment on the existence of such a module over non-domestic (skew-) Brauer graph algebra in general. The lattice of pointed modules and super-decomposable modules Here we present a sufficient condition for the existence of a super-decomposable pure- injective module in terms of independent pairs of dense chains of pointed modules. Let θ be a finitely presented Λ-module. A θ-pointed Λ-module (or, simply a pointed Λ-module) represents a pair (M;χ M) where M is a finitely presented module and χ M ∶ θ → M is a module homomorphism. If θ = Λ, then χ M is uniquely determined by χ M(1) of M. Here, identify (M;χ M) with (M;χ M(1)) and call it a pointed module. A homomorphism f ∶M →N is a pointed homomorphism from (M;m) to (N;n) if and only if f(m)=n and call f ∶(M;m)→(N;n) a pointed homomorphism.

Assume that (M;χ M) and (N;χ N) are two θ-pointed modules. A θ-pointed homo- morphism f ∶ (M;χ M) → (N;χ N) stand for a homomorphism f ∶ M → N that satisfy SUPER-DECOMPOSABLE PURE-INJECTIVE MODULES OVER SOME JACOBIAN ALGEBRAS 5 fχ M = χ N . If f ∶ M → N is an isomorphism, call f ∶ (M;χ M) → (N;χ N) a θ-pointed isomorphism. Let P Λ θ be the set of all θ-isomorphism classes of θ-pointed Λ-modules. Let ≡ be the equivalence relation on P Λ θ defined by (M;χ M)≡(N;χ N) if and only if there exist pointedhomomorphismsf ∶(M;χ M)→(N;χ N)andg ∶(N;χ N)→(M;χ M). Thequotient set PΛ θ ∶= P Λ θ / ≡ is a poset with respect to the relation ≤ defined by (M;χ M) ≤ (N;χ N) if and only if there exists a pointed homomorphism f ∶ (N;χ N) → (M;χ M). Denote by (S;χ S) the ≡-class of a θ-pointed Λ-module (S;χ S). The poset PΛ θ is a modular lattice [Pr1] with respect to the operations ⊕ and ∗ defined below. Given two pointed modules (M;χ M),(N;χ N), a pointed module (M⊕N;χ M⊕N) where χ M⊕N(l) = (χ M(l);χ N(l)) for any l ∈ θ, is defined as their pointed direct sum. Set (M;χ M)⊕(N;χ N) = (M ⊕N;χ M⊕N). Let M ∗N is the pushout of χ M and χ N , i.e, M ∗N = M ⊕N/{(χ M(l);−χ N(l)) ∶ l ∈ θ}, and ε M ∶ M → M ∗N and ε N ∶ N → M ∗N be the homomorphisms given by ε M(m)=(m;0) and ε N(n)=(0;n) for any m∈M and n∈N. Given a Q-chain C, a dense chain of θ-pointed Λ-modules is a family (M q ;χ Mq )q∈C of θ-pointed Λ-modules such that: (1) the endomorphism ring End Λ(M q) is local and χ Mq ≠0 for any q ∈C; (2) q <q′ ∈C ensures the existence of homomorphisms μ q;q ∶(M q ;χ Mq )→(M q′ ;χ M q ′); (3) q ≠q′ ∈C implies that (M q ;χ Mq ) and (M q′ ;χ M q′ ) are not isomorphic. An independent pair of dense chains of θ-pointed Λ modules is a pair ((M q ;χ Mq )q∈C1 ;(N t ;χ Nt )t∈C2 ) of dense chains of θ-pointed Λ-modules such that: (1) the endomorphism ring End Λ(M q∗N t) is local for any q ∈C 1 ,t∈C 2 ; (2) thepointedmodule(M q ;χ Mq )∗(N t ;χ Nt )isnotθ-isomorphicto(M q′ ;χ M q ′)∗(N t ;χ Nt ) or to (M q ;χ Mq )∗(N t′ ;χ N t ′) for any q ≠q′ ∈C 1 and t≠t′ ∈C 2 . Assume that the pair ((M q ;χ Mq )q∈C1 ;(N t ;χ Nt )t∈C2 ) is an independent pair of dense chains of θ-pointed Λ-modules.

It is not known whether the existence of a wide sublattice of PΛ θ (or, equivalently, a wide sublattice of the lattice of all pp-formulas over Λ) implies the existence of an independent pair of dense chains of pointed modules. The assertion (1) of the following theorem is due to Ziegler in the case of independent pairs of Q-chains in PΛ θ. Theorem 2.4. Let θ be a finitely presented module in a countable unital ring Λ. (1) If the lattice PΛ θ of θ-pointed Λ-modules has width undefined, then there exists a super-decomposable pure-injective Λ-module and KG(Λ) is infinite in this case. (2) If there exists an independent pair (L;K) of Q-chains in PΛ θ, then there exists a super-decomposable pure-injective Λ-module. 2.2. Skew group algebras: Skew group algebras were first studied in view of representation theory in [31]. Classification problem: We recall that a finite-dimensional algebra Λ over an alge- braically closed field K is tame if, for any natural number d, the set of all isomorphism classes of d-dimensional indecomposable Λ-modules can be parameterised, up to finitely SUPER-DECOMPOSABLE PURE-INJECTIVE MODULES OVER SOME JACOBIAN ALGEBRAS 9 many elements, by a finite number of regular 1-parameter families. Let μ Λ(d) be the min- imal number of 1-parameter families needed to parameterise the d-dimensional indecom- posables in the above sense. A (tame) algebra Λ is of polynomial growth (resp. domestic) [29] if there exists a natural number g such that μ Λ(d)≤dg (resp. μ Λ(d)≤g) for all d≥1 and Λ is m-domestic if m is the least such integer g. We say that Λ is tame of exponential growth if μ Λ(d)>rd for infinitely many d∈ N and some real number r >1.

Special biserial algebras: Given a quiver Q=(Q 0 ,Q 1 ,s,t) and an admissible ideal I of KQ, the algebra KQ/I is called a special biserial algebra if (1) at every vertex v in Q there are at most two arrows starting at v and there are at most two arrows ending at v; (2) for every arrow α in Q there exists at most one arrow β such that αβ ∉I and there exists at most one arrow γ such that γα∉I. If the ideal I is generated by paths of length at least two, then it is known as a string algebra. GentlealgebrasformasubclassofstringalgebrasforwhichtheidealI isgenerated by paths of length precisely two. In this case, we say (Q,I) is a gentle pair. By a word of Q, we mean a concatenation α=α 1⋯α n of symbols of Q such that t(α i)= s(α i+1) for each 1≤i<n. The source of α is defined to be s(α)=s(α 1) and the target of α is defined to be t(α)=t(α n). The inverse of α is defined to be the word α−1 =α n −1 ⋯α 1 −1. A string u is a word α = α 1⋯α n such that α i ≠ α i − + 1 1 for all 1 ≤ i ≤ n−1 and no sub-string of u or its inverse is in I. A band is defined to be a cyclic string b such that each power bm is a string, but b is not a proper power of any string. Superdecomposable modules over Brauer graph algebras Brauer graph algebras originate in the modular representation theory of finite groups, where they first appear in the form of Brauer tree algebras [14]. The author in [39, Sec- tion 4.4] compares derived equivalences of Brauer graph algebras with derived equivalences of frozen Jacobian algebras, with an interpretation of the derived equivalences of Brauer graph algebras as flips of diagonals in triangulations of marked oriented surfaces. In this section, we show the existence of superdecomposable modules over Brauer graph algebras. Given an undirected graph Γ, denote by val(v) the valency of a vertex v, i.e. the number of edges incident to v in Γ. We recall that a Brauer graph Γ is a tuple (Γ 0 ,Γ 1 ,m,o) where (Γ 0 ,Γ 1) is an unoriented graph with Γ 0 the set of vertices and Γ 1 the set of edges and ● m is a multiplicity function m∶Γ 0 → Z+, assigning a positive number to each v ∈Γ 0 . ● o is an orientation of the edges attached to each v ∈ Γ 0 . For a vertex v such that val(v) = t we denote o(v) = (x 1 ,⋯,x t). In the particular case that val(v) = 1 and the only edge attached to v is x, if m(v) = 1 the cyclic ordering is given by the single element x, but when m(v)>1 we set o(v)=(x,x) so that in the cyclic order the successor (and the predecessor) of x is x.

Following[39],givenaBrauergraphΓ,definetheBrauerquiverQ Γ =(Q 0 ,Q 1)asfollows: (1) the set of vertices Q is in bijection with the edges of Γ; 0 (2) there is an arrow x→y each time x and y are edges incident to a vertex v ∈Γ 0 and y is the immediate successor of x in the cyclic order o(v); (3) each non-distinguished vertex v of Γ with m(v)val(v) ≥ 2 gives rise an oriented cycle C v ∶ x 1 — α → 1 x 2 → ⋯x t — α → t x 1 in Q, up to cycle equivalence, established by the orientation o, i.e. if o(v)=(x 1 ,⋯,x t) (that we will set as clockwise in our examples when t>1). We say that C v is a special cycle at v. Define an ideal of relations I in KQ generated by three types of relations. For this, Γ Γ we identify the set of edges Γ of a Brauer graph Γ with the set of vertices Q of the 1 0 corresponding quiver Q and that we denote the set of vertices of the Brauer graph by Γ. Type II: C v m(v)α 1 , for any i∈Q 0 , v ∈Γ 0 and any special i-cycle C v =α 1 α 2⋯α n .
